title: Access to Electricity: Who Remains Without Power?

hero.title: Access to Electricity: Who Remains Without Power?
hero.lead: Global electricity access has expanded steadily over the past two decades, yet progress remains deeply uneven. While most regions have achieved near-universal coverage, Sub-Saharan Africa continues to lag significantly, particularly its rural communities. Initiatives like Mission 300 aim to extend electricity to 300 million more people in Africa by 2030.
hero.byline: May 2026, Story by Tony Fujs & Haruna Kashiwase, Visuals by Maarten Lambrechts

blocks.title: Electricity access is advancing globally and across all regions
blocks.subtitle: People with access to electricity, by region (% of population)
blocks.source: World Bank Group. “Access to electricity (% of population).” SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.

scenes.text: Global access to electricity has expanded steadily over the past two decades. By 2023, more than 90 percent of the world’s population had access to electricity—up from 78 percent in 2000.
Most regions already had very high levels of access in 2000, and had achieved near-universal access to electricity by 2023.
Two regions, South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa lagged noticeably in 2000.
But South Asia has since made dramatic progress, from less than 60 percent of the population in 2000 to near universal access by 2021.

Sub-Saharan Africa started from an even lower base (around 26 percent in 2000). Although the share of people with access doubled over the next two decades, only half of the region’s population had electricity by 2023.

blocks.title: There are fewer people without electricity, even as the global population grows
blocks.subtitle: Population without access to electricity
blocks.source: Energy Sector Management Assistance Program (ESMAP). SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.

scenes.text: While the global population has increased by almost 2 billion people since 2000…
… the number of people without electricity halved, falling from 1.3 billion in 2000 to 666 million in 2023.

All regions, except Sub-Saharan Africa, contributed to this rapid decline, particularly South Asia. In 2000, both South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa accounted for roughly the same share of the global population without electricity. But by 2023, South Asia achieved near-universal access, while the number of people without electricity in Sub-Saharan Africa increased. Despite significant progress in access in the region, it was not enough to keep up with population growth.
In 2023, Sub-Saharan Africa was home to 581 million people without electricity. This translates to nearly 9 in 10 of the world’s people living without electricity.
And just eight countries account for half the world’s people who still lack access to electricity.
Over one-third of the global population without electricity live in the region’s most populous nations: [emphasis: Nigeria], the [emphasis: Democratic Republic of Congo], and [emphasis: Ethiopia].

blocks.title: Ethiopia’s progress towards universal electrification outpaced Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo
blocks.subtitle: People with access to electricity (% of population)
blocks.source: World Bank staff calculations, based on Mahler, D G, Serajuddin, U, Wadhwa, D and Yonzan, N. 2026. The World Is Developing at Its Slowest Pace in 75 Years. Washington DC: World Bank Group

scenes.text: In 2015, both Ethiopia and the Democratic Republic of Congo lagged Nigeria in electricity access.
By 2023, the share of Democratic Republic of Congo’s population with access to electricity had increased only slightly, while Ethiopia had almost caught up with Nigeria.
Ethiopia is one of the countries that progressed the fastest over the 2015–2023 period. Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo, on the other hand, lagged comparable countries.
Now, explore all countries by hovering over the arrow heads or searching for a country in the drop-down menu.

blocks.html: Access is not only uneven across countries — it is also uneven within them. People in rural areas are far less likely to have electricity than those in cities.

blocks.title: Access to electricity is lower in rural areas
blocks.subtitle: People with access to electricity, by place of residence (% of population)
blocks.source: World Bank Group. “Access to electricity, rural (% of rural population),” “Access to electricity, urban (% of urban population).” SDG 7.1.1 Electrification Dataset.

scenes.text: In every region, rural access lags urban access. The gap is widest where overall access is lowest — especially Sub-Saharan Africa.

blocks.html: In all three countries with the largest populations still without electricity, rural residents lag urban residents. Ethiopia closed much of the gap; Nigeria and the DRC made limited rural progress.

blocks.title: Rapid rural progress in Ethiopia has reduced the urban–rural gap, while the Democratic Republic of Congo and Nigeria have made limited progress
blocks.subtitle: People with access to electricity, by place of residence (% of population)
blocks.source: World Bank Group. Access to electricity, rural / urban (% of population). SDG 7.1.1.

scenes.text: Ethiopia’s rural access rose sharply; Nigeria and Congo, Dem. Rep. saw little change in the rural–urban gap.

blocks.html: Nighttime lights give a more granular view of where power is actually used — and where large populations still live in the dark.

blocks.title: Nigeria: uneven and unreliable access
blocks.subtitle: Nighttime lights as a proxy for electricity access

scenes.text: [emphasis: Electricity access in Nigeria is highly uneven]. Southern zones enjoy relatively high coverage, while the north lags far behind.
[emphasis: Nighttime lights] provide a more granular view of electricity access and usage.
The brightest clusters correspond to major cities such as Lagos, Abuja, and Port Harcourt.
Let’s focus on Nigeria’s [emphasis: urban areas]…
…and zoom in on Kano. Where bright lights are detected, neighborhoods are likely well served.
Filtering out the brightest points shows large urban and suburban zones may remain underserved.
Although these are densely populated suburban settlements, they still lack highly visible illumination.
This 36 km² cell houses more than 100,000 people but remains largely in the dark — grid reliability is far from consistent.

blocks.title: Ethiopia: progress in rural areas
blocks.subtitle: Solar electricity is a key factor of progress in rural areas

scenes.text: Let’s focus on Ethiopia’s [emphasis: rural areas only] and the nighttime lights view of 2012.
The power grid reached only a few rural areas in 2012 — much rural access came through [emphasis: solar power].
The [emphasis: south-east] remained mostly in the dark in 2012.
By 2023, many more nighttime lights appear in the southeast — rapid uptake of solar electricity.
